text: The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with a left tilt. The surgeon identifies the terminal ileum and ileocolic junction, applying lateral and caudal traction to expose the ileocecal vessels. The short, fat mesentery and confined space complicate the exposure. Five centimeter swabs are used to retract intestinal loops. Adhesions between the transverse and ascending mesocolon are present, folding the hepatic flexure shut. Adhesiolysis is performed with gentle traction and counter traction to free the mesocolon.

The surgeon applies tension to the mesocolon to perform a medial to lateral dissection, starting by opening the mesenteric peritoneum widely after achieving lateral caudal traction of the ileocecal junction. Pneumodissection aids in identifying the avascular plane of Toldt, and despite excess adipose tissue, Toldt's fascia and the duodenum are successfully identified. Traction and counter-traction are used to ensure a smooth dissection, with a swab placed above the dissected duodenum to protect it and help identify the plane from the lateral side.

The surgeon carefully dissects the adipose tissue surrounding the ileocolic vessels, which are difficult to identify due to being enwrapped in fat. The vessels are located inferiorly, and tension is applied to the tissue during dissection. A mechanical vascular stapler is used to staple the two vessels simultaneously, deviating from the usual technique of applying three clips to each vessel.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection after sectioning the ileocolic vessels, carefully following the avascular embryologic plane of Toldt. A ten-centimeter swab is placed on the dissected plane to aid in lateral mobilization and protect the duodenum. The lateral dissection begins at the line of Toldt, facilitated by the thorough medial dissection and the presence of the swab, despite the extra adipose tissue.

The surgeon performs subilial plane dissection, connecting previously prepared lateral and medial planes. The dissection progresses to the mid-transverse colon, entering the lesser sac via the coloepiploic attachment. Careful identification of fat differences between the mesocolon and omentum is crucial for a bloodless separation. The coloepiploic separation is extended towards the hepatic flexure, which is partially prepared. Excess fat slows the process, but the hepatocolic ligament is identified and divided. Mobilization of the hepatic flexure continues until the previously placed swab on the duodenum is reached. Final attachments are released, and the specimen is extracted through a midline incision, followed by a stapled anisoperistaltic ileocolic anastomosis.
